name: ODE-Based Hedonic-Homeostatic Dynamical System
description: Continuous-time ordinary differential equations governing three interacting state variables: homeostatic deficit (hunger), wanting (dopaminergic incentive salience), and liking (opioid/endocannabinoid hedonic tone). Hunger drifts upward with a time constant and is reduced by eating. Wanting is driven by sensory food cues and amplified by hunger, but decays in the absence of stimuli. Liking recovers toward baseline but is depleted by repeated consumption (sensory-specific satiety) while also boosted by hedonic impact during consumption of palatable food. Action selection uses two separate drive signals: an approach drive (wanting + hunger) and an eat drive (wanting * liking + hunger). The wanting*liking product for eating ensures both motivational and hedonic components must be present, capturing the Berridge & Robinson (1998) dissociation. Integration uses forward Euler with dt=1. This formulation produces emergent behaviors including hedonic overeating, cue-triggered wanting without hunger, sensory-specific satiety, and hunger amplification of food reward.

variables.name: homeostatic_deficit
variables.description: Continuous hunger signal; rises toward 1.0 without food via a time constant, drops upon eating. Represents cumulative energy deficit.

variables.name: wanting_incentive_salience
variables.description: Dopaminergic motivational drive toward food; evolves dynamically driven by sensory cues and hunger, decays without stimulation. Represents mesolimbic dopamine activity.

variables.name: liking_hedonic_tone
variables.description: Opioid/endocannabinoid-mediated hedonic capacity; recovers toward baseline, depleted by repeated consumption (sensory-specific satiety), boosted by palatability during eating. Represents NAc shell hedonic hotspot activity.

variables.name: sensory_cue_intensity
variables.description: Strength of external food cues reaching the agent; computed from nearest food palatability decayed by distance. Represents sight/smell/anticipatory cue salience.

variables.name: eating_event
variables.description: Binary indicator: 1 if agent successfully ate last step, 0 otherwise. Drives the consumption-dependent terms in the ODEs.

variables.name: approach_drive
variables.description: Net behavioral drive for movement toward food; combining wanting and a direct hunger component

variables.name: eat_drive
variables.description: Drive to consume food; requires both wanting and liking to be present (their product), plus homeostatic pressure. Captures the liking/wanting dissociation.

variables.name: distance_to_nearest_food
variables.description: Manhattan distance to the nearest food resource

variables.name: palatability_of_nearest_food
variables.description: Palatability property of nearest food resource from env_spec

parameters.name: hunger_time_constant
parameters.source: ~20 steps to reach strong hunger; calibrated to grid-world timescale (Lutter & Nestler, 2009)

parameters.name: wanting_decay_time_constant
parameters.source: Wanting dissipates faster than hunger when cues vanish (Berridge, 2004)

parameters.name: liking_recovery_time_constant
parameters.source: Hedonic capacity recovers moderately slowly; allosthetic satiety dynamics

parameters.name: hunger_wanting_coupling
parameters.source: Hunger amplifies wanting (P4); AJPH (2014) interaction term

parameters.name: sensory_cue_wanting_coupling
parameters.source: External cues are strong drivers of wanting (Kelley & Berridge, 2002)

parameters.name: palatability_liking_coupling
parameters.source: Opioid hotspot response to taste (Berridge & Robinson, 1998)

parameters.name: satiation_rate_on_liking
parameters.source: Eating temporarily reduces liking via sensory-specific satiety mechanism

parameters.name: hunger_reduction_upon_eating
parameters.source: Partial hunger reset per eating event; reflects meal-driven satiation

parameters.name: eat_threshold
parameters.source: Agent eats when combined eat drive is sufficiently high

parameters.name: seek_threshold
parameters.source: Agent approaches food above this approach drive

parameters.name: sensory_cue_distance_decay
parameters.source: Sensory cue salience decays exponentially with distance (Kelley & Berridge, 2002)

rules.description: Sensory cue intensity: palatability of nearest food decayed exponentially by Manhattan distance
rules.pseudocode:
S = p_food * exp(-cue_decay * d_food)
# If no food visible: S = 0.0
rules.source_postulate: P1 (sensory inputs trigger hedonic circuits independently of metabolic state; Kelley & Berridge, 2002)

rules.description: Homeostatic deficit ODE: hunger drifts toward 1.0 with time constant tau_h, reduced by eating events
rules.pseudocode:
dh_dt = (1.0 - h) / tau_h - k_h * E
h = clip(h + dh_dt, 0.0, 1.0)
rules.source_postulate: P4 (energy deficit modulates hedonic system; Lutter & Nestler, 2009)

rules.description: Wanting ODE: decays without cues, driven by sensory cues, amplified by hunger*cue interaction
rules.pseudocode:
dw_dt = -w / tau_w + mu * h * S + sigma * S
w = clip(w + dw_dt, 0.0, 1.0)
rules.source_postulate: P1 (wanting can occur without hunger via sigma*S term), P4 (hunger amplifies wanting via mu*h*S term; Toates, 1986; Berridge, 2004)

rules.description: Liking ODE: recovers toward baseline, depleted by eating (sensory-specific satiety), boosted by hedonic impact of palatable food during consumption
rules.pseudocode:
dell_dt = (1.0 - ell) / tau_ell - rho * E * p_food + eta * p_food * E * (1.0 - ell)
ell = clip(ell + dell_dt, 0.0, 1.0)
rules.source_postulate: P2 (liking is dissociable from wanting; Berridge & Robinson, 1998), P6 (opioid/endocannabinoid systems mediate pleasure in hedonic hotspots)

rules.description: Approach drive: primarily wanting-driven with a smaller direct hunger contribution
Eat drive: requires both wanting AND liking (their product) plus homeostatic pressure. Captures liking/wanting dissociation: high wanting with depleted liking does not trigger eating.
rules.source_postulate: P2 (liking and wanting dissociation; Berridge & Robinson, 1998), P3 (hedonic override of satiety when both components high)

decision_logic.description: Threshold-based action selection driven by the dynamical system state. Two separate thresholds: phi_seek for approach behavior and phi_eat for consumption. The eat drive requires the product of wanting and liking, enforcing the dissociation principle. Euler integration with dt=1 advances the ODE state each step. When multiple food sources are equidistant, the agent prefers higher palatability (greedy hedonic selection).
pseudocode: # 1. Determine eating event from last step
E = 1 if (last_action was 'eat' and reward > 0) else 0
# Store p_food of consumed food for liking update
p_consumed = palatability of food that was consumed if E == 1 else 0.0
# 2. Compute sensory cue from current perception
for each f in foods: dist = abs(f.x - perception.x) + abs(f.y - perception.y)
nearest = food with minimum dist (ties broken by higher palatability)
# Use p_consumed (from last eat) for E-dependent terms in liking/hunger
dw_dt = -w / tau_w + mu * h * S + sigma * S
dell_dt = (1.0 - ell) / tau_ell - rho * E * p_for_liking + eta * p_for_liking * E * (1.0 - ell)
on_food = any(f.x == perception.x and f.y == perception.y for f in foods)
elif Phi >= phi_seek and nearest is not None:
# Move toward nearest food (greedy Manhattan-distance step)
# Low drive: random walk or stay

perception_to_variables.food_sources: perception.resources.food  # list of {x, y, type, palatability}
perception_to_variables.ate_last_step: determined in update() method: action was 'eat' and reward > 0

env_mapping.reward_source: eat action → ConsumeEffect reward of 1.0 when food is at agent position; used to detect successful eating events (E flag)

expected_behaviors.description: Hedonic overeating (P1, P3): agent eats highly palatable food even at low hunger because sensory cues drive wanting via sigma*S term
expected_behaviors.test_pseudocode: set h=0.1, place food with palatability=1.0 at agent position → S=1.0, w grows via sigma*S=0.8, Phi_eat = w*ell + 0.4*0.1 → exceeds phi_eat → agent eats despite low hunger

expected_behaviors.description: Sensory-specific satiety: repeated eating depletes liking, eventually stopping consumption even if wanting persists
expected_behaviors.test_pseudocode: place agent on food with p=0.8, force 5 consecutive eat steps → ell decreases via -rho*p_food each step → Phi_eat = w*ell drops below phi_eat → agent stops eating

expected_behaviors.description: Hunger amplification of food reward (P4): hungry agent develops much stronger wanting from same cues
expected_behaviors.test_pseudocode: compare w after 5 steps near food (d=1, p=0.7) with h=0.9 vs h=0.1 → w(h=0.9) >> w(h=0.1) due to mu*h*S term

expected_behaviors.description: Cue-triggered wanting without hunger (P1): sensory cues from palatable food drive wanting even in sated agent
expected_behaviors.test_pseudocode: set h=0.0, place food with p=1.0 at d=1 → S=1.0*exp(-0.3)=0.74, dw_dt includes sigma*0.74=0.59 → w rises from 0 → agent approaches food

expected_behaviors.description: Wanting decays without cues: if no food is visible, wanting drops to zero over ~tau_w steps
expected_behaviors.test_pseudocode: set w=0.8, remove all food → S=0, dw_dt=-w/tau_w → w decays exponentially → after 16 steps, w ≈ 0.1

expected_behaviors.description: Liking/wanting dissociation (P2): agent with high wanting but depleted liking will approach food but NOT eat
expected_behaviors.test_pseudocode: set w=0.8, ell=0.1, h=0.1 → Phi=0.8+0.03=0.83 (approach), but Phi_eat=0.08+0.04=0.12 < phi_eat → agent moves toward food but does not consume

references: Lutter & Nestler (2009) — Homeostatic and Hedonic Signals Interact in the Regulation of Food Intake — Journal of Nutrition (PMC2714382)
AJPH (2014) — Dynamic Interplay Among Homeostatic, Hedonic, and Cognitive Feedback Circuits Regulating Body Weight — doi:10.2105/AJPH.2014.301931
Berridge (2004) — Incentive theory of motivation applied to ingestive behavior — as cited in ScienceDirect S0195666322002379
Berridge & Robinson (1998) — What is the role of dopamine in reward: hedonic impact, reward learning, and incentive salience? — Brain Research Reviews (PubMed 9858756)
Toates (1986) — Incentive theory of motivation — as cited in ScienceDirect S0195666322002379
Kelley & Berridge (2002) — The Need to Feed: Homeostatic and Hedonic Control of Eating — Neuron (ScienceDirect S0896627302009698)
Volkow et al. (2011) — Hedonic mechanisms and reward hyperfunction in obesity — as cited in PMC5940344
